steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll find Orion — arguably the most recognizable constellation in the sky, visible from every inhabited place on Earth.

steps.title: Face south in winter
steps.caption: Look to the southern sky on a winter evening. In the northern hemisphere Orion rides high in the south; in the southern hemisphere he's high in the north. Best seen January through March.

steps.title: Three stars in a row
steps.caption: The Belt is unmistakable — three bright stars of nearly equal magnitude in a perfectly straight row. Nothing else in the sky looks quite like it. Alnitak, Alnilam and Mintaka span just three degrees, yet stand out from dozens of degrees away.

steps.title: The Hunter
steps.caption: With the Belt found, the rest of the Hunter falls into place. At the upper-left shoulder glows Betelgeuse (α Ori, mag 0.4) — a red supergiant nearing the end of its life. At the lower-right foot blazes Rigel (β Ori, mag 0.1) — brilliant blue-white, one of the most luminous stars in the galaxy. The colour contrast is visible to the naked eye.

steps.title: A compass for winter
steps.caption: The Belt is the most useful signpost in the winter sky. Follow it to the right (northwest) and you reach the Pleiades and Aldebaran, the red eye of Taurus. Follow it to the left (southeast) and you reach Sirius — the brightest star in the entire night sky.

steps.title: Orient yourself
steps.caption: You're somewhere in the night sky. Tonight we'll navigate to Sagittarius, a southern summer constellation best seen in July and August. Click Next to begin.

steps.title: Face south in summer
steps.caption: Turn to the southern sky on a summer night. The Milky Way arcs through this region — the bright, hazy band is our galaxy seen edge-on. Sagittarius sits right along it, near the galactic core.

steps.title: Find Scorpius first
steps.caption: Start with Scorpius — its distinctive fishhook curve is easy to recognise. Antares, the bright orange-red star at its heart, is one of the biggest stars in the sky. From Antares the tail curves down and east to a stinging pair of stars.

steps.title: Look east of Scorpius
steps.caption: Now shift your gaze to the left (east) of Scorpius's tail. Sagittarius sits right beside it — the spout of its Teapot asterism aims directly back toward Scorpius. If you can find the scorpion, the teapot is right next door.

steps.title: The Teapot asterism
steps.caption: Here's the Teapot — eight stars of 2nd and 3rd magnitude forming the outline of a teapot. The spout (three stars, left) points west toward Scorpius. The handle (two stars, right) points east. A triangular lid sits on top.

steps.title: Key stars
steps.caption: Kaus Australis (ε Sgr, mag 1.8) is the brightest star in Sagittarius — it sits at the bottom of the spout. Nunki (σ Sgr, mag 2.0) marks the top of the handle at upper right. Memorise these two and the rest follows.

steps.title: Steam from the spout
steps.caption: Pull back and look at the big picture. The 'steam' rising from the Teapot's spout is the brightest part of the Milky Way — the galactic core. Now you can find it without any guides.
